main_claims.claim: Point-particle approximation in GR breaks down at finite dynamical range because geodesic congruences focus/caustic at a finite time or spatial extent, motivating a matter horizon.
main_claims.notes: The paper invokes Raychaudhuri/geodesic-deviation reasoning, but the transition from local expansion vanishing to non-extendibility of the family of geodesics is not fully justified in the text. The assumptions on congruence, energy conditions, vorticity/shear, and initial data are not made precise enough for the claimed generality.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Sec. I; Sec. II around Eqs. (3)-(9)

main_claims.claim: Cutting spacetime at the matter horizon and gluing to an oppositely oriented sheet gives a covariant geometric backreaction term in the effective energy-momentum tensor.
main_claims.notes: This is the central construction, but the derivation is deferred to other papers, including a placeholder-looking reference Umeh:2026pm. The junction conditions, variational boundary terms, regularization, and meaning of the discrete orientation transformation are not auditable from this Letter.

main_claims.claim: Averaging the microscopic boundary/backreaction terms over many subregions yields an effective viscous fluid stress-energy tensor obeying modified conservation equations.
main_claims.notes: The averaged form is plausible as a phenomenological ansatz, but the replacement of sums over horizons and particle positions by an average is not accompanied by a controlled limit, error estimate, or assumptions on the distribution of subregions.

main_claims.claim: The backreaction density sources an additional potential whose solution produces flat or diverse galaxy rotation curves without dark matter.
main_claims.notes: The section gives a formal route from the momentum equation to an integro-differential equation and Bessel-type solutions, but important boundary conditions, parameter definitions, dimensional consistency checks, and the connection to actual galaxy data are insufficiently specified.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Precise energy, causality, regularity, and congruence assumptions under which the 'matter horizon' necessarily precedes caustics.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The finite-time/finite-distance breakdown is the motivation for cutting and gluing spacetime. Without exact hypotheses, the claimed general obstruction to point-particle modeling is too broad.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Well-defined junction conditions for gluing oppositely oriented spacetime sheets with conformally related induced metrics.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. III around Fig. 1 and Eqs. (10)-(14)
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The backreaction stress tensor is generated at the boundary; if the matching conditions or variational principle are not valid, the central mechanism fails.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Conditions under which the boundary contribution can be treated as an effective bulk stress-energy component satisfying the stated conservation equations.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A boundary/quasilocal object need not automatically behave as a local bulk fluid source in the Poisson and Euler equations used later.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Gauge and averaging prescription for the transition from microscopic backreaction terms to the single-timescale effective density.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. III Eq. (15); Sec. IV Eq. (21)
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Rotation curves depend on the radial effective density profile. Gauge or averaging artifacts could change the inferred force law.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Boundary conditions at galaxy center, matter horizon, exterior infinity, and cluster boundary sufficient to uniquely fix the Bessel-mode constants.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The flatness or growth/decay of the extra potential appears sensitive to which homogeneous mode is retained.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the companion derivations in arXiv:2604.01112 or the cited Umeh:2026pm actually prove Eqs. (12)-(14), (17)-(18), and (20) under stated hypotheses?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The provided bundle contains only this Letter, and one key reference appears as a placeholder, so the full derivation is not available here.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the focusing-to-matter-horizon argument mathematically equivalent to a known theorem, or does it require new assumptions not stated in the Letter?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The HTML source omits many equation details, and verifying the claim requires checking the exact signs, definitions, and inequalities in the original PDF or companion paper.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the rotation-curve solutions dimensionally consistent and numerically reproducible from the displayed equations and Fig. 3 parameters?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Several formula components are missing or rendered ambiguously in the provided source, and no reproducibility data or code is included.

novelty_assessment: The proposed link between GR spacetime surgery at matter horizons, an induced effective stress tensor, and galaxy rotation curves is novel relative to standard dark matter, MOND, and large-scale-structure EFT approaches. However, much of the novelty seems to reside in companion papers rather than in the submitted Letter, and the present text does not clearly distinguish rigorously derived results from phenomenological modeling choices.
score_rationale: Ambitious and potentially significant if correct, but the submitted Letter leaves multiple central mathematical steps unauditable and relies heavily on deferred derivations. The strongest concerns are the unstated hypotheses in the focusing argument, the boundary-to-bulk stress-energy construction, and the uncontrolled averaging that feeds the galaxy rotation-curve result. I score it as weakly supported rather than routine because the idea is novel, but the technical evidence in this paper is not yet strong enough.
summary: This short Letter proposes that point-particle clustering in GR should be replaced by a hierarchical spacetime-surgery construction at matter horizons, producing a geometric backreaction contribution to the effective stress-energy tensor that can flatten galaxy rotation curves. The idea is ambitious, but the submitted text largely states the key mathematical steps rather than deriving them, and several reductions needed for the rotation-curve claim are under-specified or potentially invalid. I do not certify the proof; my assessment is that the paper is interesting but not review-ready as a standalone mathematical argument.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says the action decomposition and resulting equations are given in other works, then states Eqs. (12)-(14). Reference 23 is listed as '000000', so a key cited derivation may not be publicly checkable from the bibliography. The Letter's abstract-level claim depends directly on these equations.
technical_objections.objection: The main spacetime-surgery/backreaction derivation is not self-contained enough to audit.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III, Eqs. (10)-(14), references to Umeh:2026ajv and Umeh:2026pm

technical_objections.evidence: The text states that when the bracketed terms vanish at finite time, the expansion vanishes for a finite sub-region and 'the family of geodesics within cannot be extended beyond' that time. Standard focusing statements usually require precise assumptions and often concern theta diverging to negative infinity or conjugate points, not merely theta = 0. The paper does not spell out vorticity, shear, regularity, or sign conditions enough to make this step auditable.
technical_objections.objection: The focusing argument appears to conflate vanishing local expansion or finite volume behavior with non-extendibility/caustic formation without sufficient hypotheses.

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (15) replaces a microscopic sum over particle positions and matter horizons by an average, and Eq. (21) replaces the sum with an all-sky average. No convergence assumptions, statistical homogeneity scale, window function, gauge prescription, or error estimate are given. Since the rotation-curve result depends on the radial profile of this averaged backreaction density, this is not a harmless bookkeeping step.
technical_objections.objection: The replacement of a sum over subregions/matter horizons by an all-sky average is mathematically uncontrolled.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III Eq. (15); Sec. IV Eq. (21)

technical_objections.evidence: Several quantities in the displayed formulas are undefined or only loosely described, including the parameter alpha in Eq. (21), the precise source term in Eq. (22), the functions I and K in Eq. (23), and constants fixed by matching. The text says the homogeneous limit is a modified Bessel equation after a substitution, but the displayed integro-differential equation and its Green-function-like solution are not sufficiently explicit to verify.
technical_objections.objection: The derivation of the rotation-curve equation omits enough definitions and intermediate steps that dimensional and boundary-condition checks are difficult.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states that one exterior has boundary at infinity and therefore the growing mode vanishes, while the finite cluster-boundary exterior can be approximated by the growing component. It does not derive why those are the correct global boundary conditions for real galaxies or how they connect to the earlier spacetime-sheet construction.
technical_objections.objection: The physical interpretation of the two exterior solution classes is asserted rather than derived.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. IV after Eq. (25), bullets for h- and r-hypersurfaces

technical_objections.evidence: Only a typical Hernquist-profile example is shown, with parameters and velocity dispersion choices, plus an NFW comparison. There is no fit to a sample, no accounting for baryonic mass-to-light uncertainties, no comparison to observed scatter such as the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation, and the text acknowledges that exactly flat curves may require relaxing an isothermal approximation.
technical_objections.objection: The empirical support is too thin for the claim that the mechanism naturally produces flat galaxy rotation curves.
